title: Week 15 patch cadence policy
policyName: Aggressively update public self-hosted n8n instances
acceptanceConclusion: 公開 instance 不更新就是安全邊界破洞，升級不是只有新功能。
principles: A public n8n instance is an internet-facing application, not just an automation tool.
Updates include bug fixes, security hardening, dependency fixes, permission model changes, API changes, and node safety improvements.
The minimum routine update interval for public self-hosted instances is 30 days, with faster handling for security-relevant releases.
Every production update must reuse the Week 14 backup, restore drill, image pinning, smoke test, and rollback process.
Deferring an update requires an owner, mitigation, and next review date.

examples: SSRF to cloud metadata or private network
requiredActions: Run restore drill or staging test with target image
Run smoke tests and security audit
Run smoke tests and n8n audit

examples: External secrets or project access improvement
requiredActions: Update hardening checklist if new settings apply
Update at least monthly or record deferral
Check release notes for breaking changes

examples: Update conflicts with a known breaking change
A dependency or custom node is not ready
requiredActions: Do not defer critical public exposure fixes without compensating controls

weeklyTriage: Check n8n release notes for current stable, skipped versions, breaking changes, and security relevance.
Check Docker image tag and digest for the currently pinned version and target version.
Review VPS OS packages, Docker Engine, Docker Compose, Caddy, PostgreSQL, and platform runtime updates.
Run or schedule n8n audit and review credentials, database, filesystem, nodes, and instance findings.
Review public exposure checklist for drift in HTTPS, cookies, SMTP, MFA, SSO, secrets, API, SSRF, risky nodes, community nodes, backup, and monitoring.
preUpdateGate: Restore drill or staging test completed
securityBoundaryStatement: A public n8n instance exposes login, webhooks, APIs, OAuth callbacks, workflow data, credentials usage, and possibly user-installed code. If the instance is not updated, the public security boundary remains stuck at old assumptions while threats and fixes move on.
